title: Poems from the Garrett MS
summary: CME00084 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan encoding of the four poem texts on Robert Kilburn Root's printed pp. 365-379, selected from his article 'Poems from the Garrett MS' in Englische Studien 41 (1910), pp. 360-379. Root says the text is, apart from punctuation, an exact transcription of Princeton University Library, Garrett MS 143; he leaves scribal errors uncorrected, expands contractions in italics, and supplies stanza spacing for the final three lyrics. The manuscript is a 51-leaf devotional miscellany made in England around the beginning of the fifteenth century; the four poems occupy current fols. 38v-51v. Princeton now exposes a complete 118-canvas full-color sequence containing covers, pastedowns, twelve flyleaf surfaces, and every manuscript leaf. The whole sequence and Princeton's generated 119-page PDF are cached locally, with the four-poem portion mapped to IIIF canvases 84-110 and PDF pages 85-111. A complete 480-page scan of Englische Studien 41 and the complete DIMEV 1.0 release are also cached. DIMEV identifies the four intellectual works as records 2777, 5311, 925, and 926 (IMEV 1653, 3371, 562, and 563) and documents their wider witness traditions without making those collateral copies sources of Root's text.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00084
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00084; the file is 32,776 bytes.
The XML contains four DIV1 poem units, 522 L elements, 37 LG elements, 15 printed-page breaks for pp. 365-379, 77 line or folio milestones, and seven editorial notes.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00084
entities.description: A public keyed transcription of Root's four poem texts on pp. 365-379. It retains the printed page and manuscript-folio milestones, lineation, footnotes, and Root's italic expansion typography, but omits the article's manuscript description and textual discussion on pp. 360-364.

entities.label: Robert Kilburn Root, 'Poems from the Garrett MS' (1910)
entities.description: The immediate print source. Root introduces Garrett MS 143 and its contents on pp. 360-361, compares the four poems with other witnesses on pp. 361-364, and prints the Garrett texts on pp. 365-379. CME00084 encodes the four printed texts, not the complete article.

bibliographic.citation: Robert Kilburn Root, 'Poems from the Garrett MS,' Englische Studien 41 (1910): 360-379.
bibliographic.title: Poems from the Garrett MS

notes: Root's article is signed at Princeton and identifies the manuscript as then deposited in Princeton University Library.

Root's folio-side notation uses a/b. The current Princeton surrogate uses recto/verso and places the first poem's rubric and opening on fol. 38v; Root's first in-text folio milestone occurs after line 19 at fol. 39a.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Englische Studien volume 41 (1910)
entities.description: A complete 480-page PDF of the journal volume. Root's article occupies PDF pages 376-395; the four poem texts encoded in CME00084 occupy PDF pages 381-395.

entities.label: Princeton University Library, Garrett MS 143
alternate_labels: A treatise on the ten commandments. MS.
entities.description: A small composite devotional miscellany containing prose and verse in Middle English and Latin. Root prints its final four texts, at current fols. 38v-51v.

date_statements.label: Production
date_statements.value: Around the beginning of the fifteenth century (Princeton display date 1400; Root: apparently first half of the fifteenth century)

physical_description.extent: 51 leaves, with three front and three rear flyleaves represented in the digital surrogate
physical_description.dimensions: 140 x 95 mm (current catalog); Root measured the written volume at 97 x 131 mm and the ruled space at 59 x 89 mm
notes: Princeton identifies the script as textualis semi-quadrata and describes blue two- to three-line initials with red flourishing.
The manuscript has 20 lines per page according to Root.
The nineteenth-century English binding by Francis Bedford is brown morocco over pasteboards; its spine title is 'A treatise on the ten commandments. MS.'
Princeton's contents list gives the exact target loci: Trental of St Gregory, ff. 38v-44v; Saying the Best, ff. 44v-46v; Thank God of All, ff. 47r-49r; Making Amends, ff. 49r-51v.
The four poems are the codex's final contents; the complete physical witness also includes eight earlier devotional prose items on ff. 1r-38r.

entities.label: Princeton complete digital facsimile of Garrett MS 143
entities.description: A 118-canvas full-color sequence exposing the front cover, front pastedown, six front-flyleaf surfaces, fols. 1r-51v, six rear-flyleaf surfaces, back pastedown, and back cover. Princeton's generated PDF adds a rights-and-citation page before the 118 images.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 image sequence and generated PDF
notes: The catalog calls the access route a 'Digitized microfilm copy,' but the current IIIF images are visibly full color. The precise relationship between that legacy label and the current image provenance is unresolved.
The published sequence does not expose separate spine or edge photographs.

entities.label: DIMEV records for the four poems in CME00084
entities.description: The version 1.0 Digital Index of Middle English Verse records that establish four separate intellectual works, identify the Garrett loci and Root page ranges, and enumerate their wider manuscript and edition traditions.
notes: Record 2777 lists six witnesses of The Trental of St Gregory; record 5311 lists two witnesses of All Way Fond to Say the Best; record 925 lists seven witnesses of Thank God of All; and record 926 lists four witnesses of For Thy Sins Amends Make.
The collateral witnesses documented by DIMEV belong to the wider traditions. They are not modeled as sources of Root's Garrett transcription.

subject.description: All four encoded DIV1 poem units

object.description: The four printed poem texts, excluding Root's pp. 360-364 introduction

subject.description: The four poem texts printed on pp. 365-379

object.description: The four final verse works in Garrett MS 143

assertion.notes: Root explicitly calls the printed text, save for punctuation, an exact transcript of the manuscript.

subject.description: Root article inside the complete scanned volume

object.description: Every surface exposed by Princeton for Garrett MS 143

scope.notes: The sequence includes covers and pastedowns but no separately photographed spine or edges.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A 51-leaf devotional miscellany containing eight prose items followed by four verse items
editorial_practices.representation: An article introduction followed by the four complete verse texts only
editorial_practices.description: Root prints the four verse pieces that conclude the manuscript and does not print the eight preceding prose works.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript wording, scribal errors, contractions, punctuation, and unmarked stanza boundaries
editorial_practices.representation: Uncorrected text with editorial punctuation, italic expansions, and supplied stanza spacing
editorial_practices.description: Root says the text is exact except for punctuation, deliberately leaves even scribal slips uncorrected, expands contractions in italics, and spaces the final three lyrics into stanzas where the manuscript generally uses paragraph signs, sometimes omitted.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Root's italic expansions of manuscript contractions
editorial_practices.description: The encoding preserves Root's printed italic expansion typography. The italics represent editorially expanded contractions, not manuscript emphasis or a facsimile of the abbreviation signs.

editorial_practices.representation: Only the poem texts on pp. 365-379
editorial_practices.description: The encoding excludes Root's codicological description and comparison of witnesses on pp. 360-364 while retaining all four transcribed poem texts.

local_copies.label: Complete 118-canvas Princeton facsimile of Garrett MS 143 at 1800-pixel width

work_portion.label: The four poems in CME00084 within complete Garrett MS 143
notes: Poem I: fols. 38v-44v, canvases 84-96; poem II: fols. 44v-46v, canvases 96-100; poem III: fols. 47r-49r, canvases 101-105; poem IV: fols. 49r-51v, canvases 105-110.
The first and second poems share fol. 44v / canvas 96; the third and fourth share fol. 49r / canvas 105.
Complete for every provider-exposed surface: front and back covers, pastedowns, six front and six rear flyleaf surfaces, and fols. 1r-51v. Princeton exposes no separate spine or edge views.
All 118 images were retrieved with effective IIIF width requests of 1800,; the exact provider manifest and per-image checksums and URLs are retained in the bundle.

Visual inspection confirms a full-color sequence preserving binding color, parchment tone, red rubrics and flourishing, and blue initials, notwithstanding the catalog's 'Digitized microfilm copy' label.

work_portion.label: The four poems in CME00084 within the complete 119-page PDF

The 119-page PDF was generated by Princeton on 2026-07-13 UTC and retains the complete 118-image sequence after its rights page.
PDF signature, page count, checksum, byte size, rights page, target-page mapping, and complete first/final surfaces were audited.

access.format: Versioned XML data release in ZIP

rights.basis: The texts are attested in medieval manuscripts, including Garrett MS 143 around the beginning of the fifteenth century.
notes: This does not determine rights or contractual terms for Root's editorial matter, the U-M encoding, catalog descriptions, manuscript photographs, or interface assets.

rights.component: 1910 article text, transcription, and editorial matter
University of Michigan digital text and collection item
rights.basis: The Corpus of Middle English collection applies the RightsStatements.org No Copyright - United States statement to its public items.
notes: The assertion is jurisdiction-specific and is not restated as a worldwide dedication.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and item presentation
rights.basis: Internet Archive item metadata records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and reports no visible copyright notice for the 1910 volume.
notes: This is a provider metadata assertion, not an affirmative open-content license for every scan-layer asset.

rights.component: Princeton manuscript photographs, IIIF derivatives, generated PDF, and item presentation
rights.jurisdiction: Unspecified; provider terms and applicable law
rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest applies Copyright Not Evaluated. Princeton's generated PDF says the Library claims no copyright or license governing the digital resource, but describes provision as non-commercial open access for fair-use academic and research purposes only and requests citation.
notes: Public viewing and downloading do not establish permission to redistribute the facsimile in the Git repository or to use its images in commercial print-on-demand products.
Keep the cached bytes Git-ignored unless Princeton supplies clearer written terms authorizing the intended redistribution or publication use.
The public URLs, exact target mapping, bibliographic facts, and observations derived from study of the images can be cited without placing the image files in Git.

rights.basis: The versioned release includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license.

agents.name: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the repository artifact, four encoded poem units, 522 L and 37 LG elements, printed pp. 365-379, Root's manuscript folio milestones, notes, italic expansions, identifiers, and 2016 revision statement.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, 'Poems from the Garrett MS,' item CME00084 and collection browse record.
evidence.summary: Supports the public digital item identity, Root credit, Englische Studien 41 (1910) source, public access, and collection-level No Copyright - United States assertion.

evidence.citation: Robert Kilburn Root, 'Poems from the Garrett MS,' Englische Studien 41 (1910), 360-379.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the article title, author, publication, manuscript description and contents, exact source statement, punctuation policy, uncorrected errors, italic expansions, stanza spacing, four poem texts, folio milestones, comparison witnesses, and poem-specific printed page ranges.
Supports the exact item identifier, complete-volume PDF route, 484 scanned images, Toronto source, and Internet Archive's NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT metadata assertion for the 1910 source volume.

evidence.citation: Princeton University Library, '[English devotional miscellany],' Garrett MS 143, catalog record 99108881333506421 and Digital PUL record.
evidence.summary: Supports the current shelfmark, title, 51-leaf parchment extent, display date 1400, England, Middle English and Latin languages, composite-codex description, script, decoration, binding, provenance, exact twelve-item contents and loci, holding, catalog URL, digital-object route, and Copyright Not Evaluated label.

evidence.citation: Princeton Figgy IIIF Presentation manifest for Garrett MS 143.
evidence.summary: Enumerates all 118 canvases, their folio labels, Image API services, and the Copyright Not Evaluated URI; establishes complete provider-surface coverage and the exact four-poem mapping at canvases 84-110.

evidence.citation: Princeton-generated complete PDF for Garrett MS 143, rights and citation page.
evidence.summary: States Copyright Not Evaluated; says Princeton claims no copyright or license governing the resource; describes provision as free, non-commercial open access for fair-use academic and research purposes only; requests citation; and identifies Garrett MS 143.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of representative and boundary images in Princeton's complete Garrett MS 143 IIIF sequence.
evidence.summary: Confirms the four poem boundaries and shared folio surfaces, rubric and initial treatment, paragraph signs rather than consistent blank stanza spacing, and full-color capture of binding, parchment, red and blue decoration despite the catalog's 'digitized microfilm copy' wording.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records 2777, 5311, 925, and 926; bibliography entry Root1910; manuscript entry PULGar143.
evidence.summary: Supports the four work identities and IMEV numbers, preferred and manuscript titles, subjects, verse forms, incipits and explicits, Garrett loci, Root page ranges, and the wider witness and edition censuses.

evidence.citation: Local checksum, hard-link, PDF-page-count, image-dimension, request-profile, member-count, and ZIP-integrity audit of source-cache/CME00084.
evidence.summary: Verified four cached files and exact checksums and byte sizes; true hard-link reuse of DIMEV 1.0; the 118-image complete Princeton bundle; the 119-page Princeton PDF; valid 1800, IIIF width requests; the complete 480-page journal scan; target mappings; and ZIP integrity.

review_notes: The cataloging subject is Root's four-poem printed selection, not one medieval work, the complete article, or the complete manuscript.
The whole manuscript was cached before mapping the work portion; shared boundary surfaces at fols. 44v and 49r are included in both adjacent poem mappings.
Root's a/b folio notation, Princeton's current recto/verso labels, and PDF/IIIF positions are retained as distinct locator systems.
DIMEV's wider witness census is recorded as scholarly context without retroactively making collateral witnesses sources of Root's transcription.

open_questions.question: Why does Princeton label the online object a 'Digitized microfilm copy' when the current 118-canvas sequence is visibly full color?
next_steps: Ask Princeton whether the current IIIF sequence replaced an earlier microfilm surrogate or whether the catalog label is legacy wording.
Continue to describe the current images from direct visual inspection rather than inferring monochrome reproduction from the label.
Record any repository-supplied imaging provenance and capture date.

open_questions.question: Do Princeton's terms permit redistribution of the manuscript facsimile in the Git repository or use of its images in commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand publications?
next_steps: Ask Princeton Special Collections for written clarification covering public Git redistribution, scholarly quotation, and commercial or noncommercial publication.
Cite the public facsimile and use it for research, but keep cached image and PDF bytes Git-ignored unless the intended redistribution is expressly authorized.
Preserve separately the Copyright Not Evaluated statement and the generated PDF's non-commercial fair-use wording.

open_questions.question: When and where were the four constituent poems composed, and how should the manuscript versions be related to their variant traditions?
next_steps: Consult current poem-specific studies for IMEV 1653, 3371, 562, and 563.
Keep composition separate from Garrett MS 143's English production around the beginning of the fifteenth century.
Do not infer one author, composition date, or composition region for Root's artificial four-poem selection.

open_questions.question: How exactly should Root's a/b folio milestones be crosswalked to the current Princeton recto/verso labels at the opening of the first poem?
next_steps: Retain Princeton's visually verified fol. 38v opening and DIMEV's fol. 38v locus as the current physical mapping.
Treat Root's first internal milestone at 39a after line 19 as a locator within the poem rather than its opening.
Ask Princeton whether any historical foliation or digitization note explains the presentation.
